coredata.dc:title: CeO2 quantum dots for highly selective and ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of 4-nitrophenol via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Hongqiang
Wang, Hailong
Lu, Yixia
Zeng, Yanbo
Yang, Yiwen
Zhang, Zulei
Wang, Hongmei
Wang, Xiao
Li, Lei
coredata.dc:description: A rapid and simple fluorescence probe based on CeO2 quantum dots (QDs) was developed for highly selective and ultrasensitive direct determination of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). CeO2 QDs were prepared using the sol-gelmethod with the precursor of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O as a cerium source. The products were characterized through high-resolution electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. The fluorescent probe based on CeO2 QDs exhibited a broad linear response to the concentration of 4-NP in the range of 0.005–75.00 μM and provided a low detection limit of 1.50 nM. The fluorescence of CeO2 QDs was quenched by 4-NP through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism owing to the well overlaps between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CeO2 QDs with the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of 4-NP. This result was confirmed by the time-resolved fluorescence spectra and the evaluation of the interaction distance between CeO2 QDs and 4-NP. The prepared CeO2 QDs are successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in real water samples, where the spiked recoveries range from 98.2% to 102.4%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CeO2
Quantum dots
Fluorescent detection
4-Nitrophenol
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization of CeO2 QDs
Optical properties and stability of CeO2 QDs
Possible sensing mechanism of CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characterization of CeO2 QDs: (A) TEM image, (B) size distribution from TEM analysis, and (C) High resolution-TEM image of CeO2 QDs.
Chemical characterization of CeO2 QDs: (A) XRD patterns, and (B) Raman spectra.
Chemical characterization of CeO2 QDs: (A) XPS survey full spectra, (B) Ce 3d, (C) O 1 s, and (D) C 1 s peaks signals from XPS analysis.
Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectra of CeO2 QDs excited at different wavelengths from 220 nm to 270 nm at an interval of 5 nm.
Effect of (A) pH, (B) response time, and (C) temperature on the fluorescence quenching capacities (ΔF = F 0-F) of CeO2 QDs in the presence of 4-NP (5.0 μM).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CeO2 QDs in the presence of various concentrations of 4-NP. (B) The plot of fluorescence quenching value (F0/F-1) at λex 255 nm versus the concentrations of 4-NP (0.005–75.00 μM).
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CeO2 QDs, 4-NP (100 μM), and their mixtures. (B) Normalized absorption spectrum of 4-NP (100 μM), fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of CeO2 QDs.
Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of CeO2 QDs in the absence and presence of 4-NP (5.0 μM) at λex of 255 nm.

(A) Selectivity of CeO2 QDs for 4-NP over the structural analogs and the inorganic salts under the same concentrations (5.0 μM). The inset is the chemical structures of 4-NP, the analogs, and the kinds of inorganic salts. (B) The overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of the analogs and the excitation and emission spectra of CeO2 QDs.
Comparison of the performance of CeO2 QDs for 4-NP determination with the previously reported fluorescence probes.
Quantitative determination of 4-NP in the real water using CeO2 QDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: CeO2 quantum dots for highly selective and ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of 4-nitrophenol via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing University, Jiahang Road 118, Jiaxing 314001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing University, Jiahang Road 118, Jiaxing 314001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescentprobehas been developed for the rapid, simple, ultrasensitive, and highly selective determination of 4-nitrophenol based on thefluorescence resonance energy transfer betweenCeO2 quantum dotsand 4-nitrophenol.

A rapid and simple fluorescence probe based on CeO2 quantum dots (QDs) was developed for highly selective and ultrasensitive direct determination of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). CeO2 QDs were prepared using the sol-gelmethod with the precursor of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O as a cerium source. The products were characterized through high-resolution electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. The fluorescent probe based on CeO2 QDs exhibited a broad linear response to the concentration of 4-NP in the range of 0.005–75.00 μM and provided a low detection limit of 1.50 nM. The fluorescence of CeO2 QDs was quenched by 4-NP through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism owing to the well overlaps between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CeO2 QDs with the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of 4-NP. This result was confirmed by the time-resolved fluorescence spectra and the evaluation of the interaction distance between CeO2 QDs and 4-NP. The prepared CeO2 QDs are successfully applied to the determination of 4-NP in real water samples, where the spiked recoveries range from 98.2% to 102.4%.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) has been widely used in the manufacturing of pharmaceuticals, pesticides, dyes, and explosives [1]. 4-NP exhibitsgood chemical stability and high water solubility due to its high conjugate structure and hydroxylgroup, resulting in thegradual accumulationin environmental water. Research has shown that human exposure to 4-NP is mainly through the respiratorytract, gastrointestinal tract,andskin, leading to the damage of the cardiovascular, mucousmembrane, and central nervous system [2]. Consideringitscumulative toxicity, the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency lists 4-NP as an environmental pollutant and the permitted maximum concentration in drinking water is approximately 0.43 μM [3]. Accordingly, establishing a simple, high sensitivity and selectivity method is highly desired to determine 4-NP in environmental water.

Various analytical techniques, including the electrochemical method [4–6], chromatographic technique [7,8], capillary electrophoresis [9,10], and spectrofluorimetric assays [11,12], have been developed for 4-NP detection in environmental water. In particular, fluorescence methods have increasingly aroused extensive attention due to their simple operation, fast response, and high sensitivity. A variety of fluorescence methods involving diverse fluorescence materials with excellent physical and chemical properties, such as organicfluorescence dyes [13], fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) [14], and metal-organic framework-based materials [15], have been established for the detection of 4-NP. Among different fluorescence materials, QDs have attracted considerable interest and extensively applied as sensor platforms. QDs are fluorescent inorganic semiconductor nanocrystals, and their diameters are typically less than 10 nm. QDs present unique electrical and optical properties due to the quantum sizeand dielectric confinementeffect. Compared to organicfluorescence dyes, QDs have many significant advantages, such as adjustable fluorescence emission, high quantum yield, and anti-bleachingability [16]. The energy transfer between QDs and the analytes can be extremely efficient, which is a factor for the high sensitivity of QDs probes [17].

To date, numerous types of QDs, such as carbon dots (CDs) [18], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [19], silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) [20], CdTe QDs [21], and CdSe/ZnS core/shell structured QDs [22], have been prepared and used in the detection of 4-NP. However, all these QDs have some limitations. CDs and GQDs have low quantum yields (QY). Although the QY can be improved by surface modification or heteroatom doping, the preparation is complicated and the yield is very low [23,24]. For Cd-based QDs, The toxicity of cadmium ions resulting from the degeneration of QDs limits its widespread use [25]. Hence, developing a low-cost and environment-friendly nano-optical material for the determination of 4-NP is crucial.

With the increasing interest in nanoparticles, metal oxides are relatively attractive for chemical sensors because they possess appealing physicochemical properties and excellent biocompatibility [26]. Hence, we synthesized novel CeO2 QDs via the sol-gelmethod for the determination of 4-NP. These CeO2 QDs can be directly used as a highly selective and ultrasensitive chemical probe toward 4-NP. Further experiments demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of 4-NP toward CeO2 QDs is attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. These experimental evidences completely fulfilled the requirements for the FRET mechanism that spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the energy donor and the absorption spectrum of the energy acceptor, the fluorescence lifetime of the energy donor decreases after adding the energy acceptor, and the donor-acceptor distance should be in the range of 1.0–10 nm [27–29]. This developed method was then successfully utilized in the probing of 4-NP in river and lake water with satisfactory results. This finding indicates that the as-obtained CeO2 QDs have potential application in the determination of 4-NP in real water.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), hydrogen peroxide solution (30%), ammonia solution (28.0–30.0%), urea, 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), 2,5-dichlorophenol (2,5-DCP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2, 4, 6-TCP), phloroglucinol (THB), acetaminophen (APAP) and 1-naphthol (1-NaPh) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Nitric acid (65.0–68.0%), anhydrous ethanol, polyvinyl alcohol 1750 ± 50(PVA), and all inorganic salts used in this study were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All the reagents are analytical grade and used without any purifications.

The morphology of the CeO2 QDs was investigated on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired from an XRD-7000 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) mapping were examined on a DXRxi microscopic Raman imaging spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and an Escalab 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. The absolute quantum yields and the fluorescence lifetime of CeO2 QDs were measured on an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) with an integrating sphere, respectively. All the fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectra were recorded on an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a UV-vis Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA), respectively.

The CeO2 QDs were prepared using the sol-gelmethod according to a previous report with some modifications [30]. Specifically, under vigorous stirring, 1.0 mL hydrogen peroxide solution (30%) was added dropwise into 20 mL Ce(NO3)3 aqueous solution (1.0 mol/L), and the solution color changed from initially colorless to bright yellow and light brown. Then, 3.0 mL ammonia solution (28.0–30.0%) was added rapidly, and reddish-brown flocculentprecipitation was generated. The mixture was then heated to reflux, and the color gradually turned into light yellow. After this treatment, the mixture was filtered, and the precipitate was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water and adjusted the pH value to 1.0 with a nitric acid aqueous solution (v/v: 1:5). The mixture was then heated to reflux until the color became yellow-green. PVA aqueous solution (2 mL, 10 m/v %) and urea aqueous solution (2 mL, 5 m/v %) were dripped slowly, boiled for 30 min, then, filtered and washed with deionized water. The precipitation was finally dispersed in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol and stored at 4 °C.

The procedure for fluorescence determination of 4-NP was as follows: the solution of CeO2 QDs (1.0 μL mL−1) was prepared through dispersion in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 6.0). The stability time was 8.0 min. Then, CeO2 QDs solution (2.0 mL) was added into a plastic centrifuge tube (5.0 mL). Subsequently, different concentrations of 4-NP solution were added, and the total volume of the mixtures was kept at 4.0 mL. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after 1.0 min equilibration at room temperature. The selectivity experiment of the CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP was conducted by mixing other potential interfering compounds rather than 4-NP with the same concentrations (5.0 μM). The river and the lake water samples collected from Jiaxing City were filtered with 0.22 μm membranes to remove the suspendedsubstances and diluted 100 times with deionized water before use. After the above treatment, there is no 4-NP detected. The examined water samples were spiked with different concentrations (0.050, 0.500, and 5.000 μM) of standard 4-NP solutions. The recovery experiments were consistent with the above-mentioned procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization of CeO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphology of CeO2 QDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs. The uniform distribution of CeO2 QDs is imaged in Fig. 1 A, and the diameters are distributed in the range of 1.0–4.0 nm with the highest percentage range from 2.0 nm to 3.0 nm (Fig. 1B). Following the statistical analysis of more than 100 particles, the average size of CeO2 QDs observed is 2.4 nm. The HR-TEM image of CeO2 QDs (Fig. 1C) demonstrates clear lattice structures, and the lattice parameter is 0.31 nm, which can be attributed to the (111) diffraction planes of the CeO2 QDs.

The measured XRD patterns of CeO2 QDs are presented in Fig. 2 A. Five diffraction peaks located at 28.5°, 47.5°, 56.2°, 69.2°, and 76.6° can be ascribed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), and (331) planes of the cubic fluorite structure of CeO2. These planes are well-matched with those reported in JCPDS card 34-0394. Furthermore, all the reflection peaks are weak and broad, which are the characteristics of QD nanomaterials. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the Raman spectroscopy of CeO2 QDs exhibits an intensive peak at the center of 452.8 cm−1. It can be assigned to the symmetrical stretching vibration of the Ce-O bond. The relatively large half-peak width of the Raman peak is associated with a small crystallite size. These observations are consistent with the results of HR-TEM and XRD spectra.

The chemical compositions and electronic states of CeO2 QDs were examined by employing XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum of CeO2 QDs shown in Fig. 3 A indicates that the composite mainly consists of Ce, O, and C elements. The Ce 3d XPS spectrum of CeO2 QDs (Fig. 3B) presents four main peaks arising from Ce4+ and Ce3+ contributions. The binding energy peaks centered at 898.4 and 916.8 eV correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 states of Ce4+ ions, and the peaks at 882.7 and 901.1 eV are originated from 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 states of Ce3+ ions, respectively. Two more satellite peaks are present at 887.8 and 907.6 eV. These results are in agreement with the previous researches on CeO2 nanoparticles [31,32]. It is noted that the O 1 s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3C shows two peaks centered at 529.5 and 532.5 eV, which are corresponded to the lattice oxygen from the crystal structure of CeO2 and the hydroxyl oxygen from the hydroxyl group of the stabilizers (PVA), respectively. In Fig. 3D, the XPS spectrum of C 1 s orbital was deconvolved to different chemical environments of carbon. The two peaks at 284.5 and 296.0 eV can be assigned to the C = C and C-C bonds of the stabilizers (PVA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of CeO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 4 , CeO2 QDs exhibit strong fluorescence due to their unique quantum size effect. The fluorescence emission intensity first increases and then decreases with an increasing excitation wavelength from 220 to 270 nm. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are centered at 255 nm and 350 nm, respectively.

The absolute quantum yield of the prepared CeO2 QDs was measured to be 13.80%. Furthermore, the emission peak position of CeO2 QDs locates at the same position. This phenomenon can be attributed to the narrow size distribution of as-synthesized CeO2 QDs.

To investigate the photostability of CeO2 QDs, the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs toward pH, time, and temperature were investigated. The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated with 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution from 4.0 to 10.0. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs has almost no change at the pH value from 4.0 to 6.0, then decreased rapidly with the pH value increasing from 7.0 to 10.0. This result might be due to the decomposition of CeO2 QDs in the basic condition. Fig. S1B showed the variation of fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs with time. The results indicated the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged after 8.0 min. Therefore, the stability time of 8.0 min at pH 6.0 was selected. The temperature has a minor effect on the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs below 40 °C (Fig. S1C), which indicated that CeO2 QDs have good photostability at room temperature. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs during the storage was also investigated. As shown in Fig.S2, when CeO2 QDs were stored at 4 °C for 30 days, the intensity slightly changed, indicating that CeO2 QDs have excellent storage-stability.

To achieve the optimum analytical condition, experimental parameters such as pH, response time, and temperature were investigated. Tris-HCl buffer solutions (10 mM) with different pH were selected to investigate the effect of pH on the quenching capacity. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the ΔF values increase gradually in the pH range of 4.0–6.0, then decrease sharply within the pH of 6.0–8.0. Accordingly, a pH of 6.0 was adopted. The response time of CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP was evaluated and shown in Fig. 5B. The ΔF values kept almost unchanged after 1.0 min, therefore, a fixed response time of 1.0 min was selected in the further experiments. The quenching capacity of 4-NP on CeO2 QDs was also investigated with temperature (Fig. 5C). The ΔF values keep nearly unchanged in the range of temperature from 20 °C to 40 °C. Thus, the analytical experiments were conducted at room temperature.

To investigate the performance of CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP. The fluorescence spectra of CeO2 QDs varying with the concentrations of 4-NP are scanned and depicted in Fig. 6 A. It revealed that the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs reduces gradually with the increase of 4-NP concentrations. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs and the concentration of 4-NP is in agreement with the Stern-Volmer equation, which can be expressed by the formula F0 /F = 1 + K sv C 4-NP, here F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities of CeO2 QDs with and without 4-NP, respectively. K sv is the Stern-Volmer constant which is related to the quenching efficiency, and C 4-NP is the molar concentration of 4-NP. As shown in Fig. 6B, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) was obtained from 0.005 μM to 75.00 μM with the fitted linear equation of F 0/F = 1.14 + 0.059 C 4-NP. The detection limit (LOD) value calculated with a formula of 3σ/K is 1.50 nmol/L. As shown in Table 1 , it can be seen that the LOD of the prepared fluorescence probe is lower than the most reported methods for 4-NP detection based on the fluorescence of QDs. Therefore, this developed fluorescence method has a wide linear range and a low LOD, showing good potential in the determination of 4-NP in watersamples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible sensing mechanism of CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, fluorescence quenching of semiconductor QDs can either attributed to an electron transfer or an energy transfer mechanism [39,40]. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing ability of the nitro group, 4-NP plays as a good electron acceptor. Hence, fluorescence quenching of 4-NP is believed to occur by a charge transfer mechanism. The UV-vis absorption spectra of CeO2 QDs, 4-NP, and their mixtures were further measured and shown in Fig. 7 A. The shape of the spectrum of the mixtures is extremely similar to that of 4-NP without any new absorption band generation at a long wavelength. These results indicate that there is no complex and no charge transfer between CeO2 QDs and 4-NP. This fact is possibly derived from the stabilizers (PVA), which capped the CeO2 QDs, and suppressed the interaction between CeO2 and 4-NP, although the hydroxyl group of 4-NP can form hydrogen bonds with the oxygen on the surface of CeO2 QDs. Therefore, electron transfer is not the quenching mechanism in this study.

On the other hand, FRET is also considered to be a predominant quenching process of luminescent QDs. One requirement for a FRET mechanism is that there must be a spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the quencher, and the energy transfer efficiency depends on the extent of spectral overlap [39]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the absorption spectrum around 320 nm of 4-NP has considerable spectral overlap with the emission spectrum centered at 350 nm of CeO2 QDs, resulting in the high fluorescence quenching of CeO2 QDs via a FRET mechanism. To confirm the FRET mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence lifetime decay measurements were conducted. Fig. 8 shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of CeO2 QDs in the absence and presence of 4-NP. The average lifetime of CeO2 QDs decreases from 7.14 ns to 5.35 ns with the addition of 5.0 μM 4-NP, which provides further evidence to support the FRET quenching mechanism. As a final check of the FRET mechanism, the donor-acceptor distance (r) between CeO2 QDs and 4-NP was estimated, the distance (r) was deduced to be 1.93 nm, which fulfills the requirement of the FRET mechanism that the donor-acceptor distance should be in the range of 1.0 nm–10.0 nm. The detailed calculations of the distance are described in Supporting Information. Accordingly, all these results strongly confirm that the fluorescence quenching of CeO2 QDs induced by 4-NP is predominantly attributed to a FRET mechanism.

The quenching efficiencies of some structural analogs of 4-NP, including 2-NP, 3-NP, 2,4-DCP, 2,5-DCP, 2, 4, 6-TCP, THB, APAP, and 1-NaPh, were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 9 A, only 4-NP exhibits an obvious quenching effect towards the fluorescence of CeO2 QDs, whereas the analogs have an ignorable or slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs. 4-NP can be highly and selectively recognized from 2-NP and 3-NP. The UV-vis absorption spectra of these analogs were measured to illustrate the good selectivity of CeO2 QDs toward 4-NP. Weaker spectral overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of CeO2 QDs has been observed from the spectra of 2,4-DCP, 2,5-DCP, TCP, and THB (Fig. 9B). The results indicate that these analogs induce minimal fluorescence quenching of CeO2 QDs. 2-NP, 3-NP, and 1-Naph have a minimal effect on the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs due to the slight spectral overlap between their absorption spectra and the excitation and emission spectra of CeO2 QDs. The results of the UV-vis absorption spectrum of APAP indicate a considerable overlap with the excitation spectrum of CeO2 QDs, which induces an observable fluorescence quenching of CeO2 QDs mainly through an inner-filter effect mechanism [41,42]. The effect of an inorganic salt such as NaCl, KCl, CuSO4, FeCl3, CoCl2, and MgSO4 on the fluorescence response of CeO2 QDs was also investigated using the concentration of 5.0 μM. As shown in Fig. 9A, all these inorganic salts have a minor impact on the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs. In addition, oxygen has an insignificant impact on the fluorescence intensity of CeO2 QDs. it can be deduced that the potential interferences do not affect the fluorescence determinationof4-NP basedonCeO2 QDs.

To evaluate the actual applicability of the probe in practical water samples, the CeO2 QDs were utilized to detect 4-NP in the river and lake water collected from Jiaxing city. Table 2 shows that 4-NP is not detected in the real water samples. Then, different concentrations (0.050, 0.500, and 5.000 μM) of the standard 4-NP solutions are spiked into the above-mentioned water samples using the standard addition method. The spiked recoveries of 4-NP are between 98.2% and 102.4%, and the RSDs (n = 3) vary from 1.6% to 4.4%. This finding indicates that the proposed method has a good recovery, accuracy, and repeatability. Therefore, the fluorescence probe based on CeO2 QDs has a potential value to determinate 4-NP in environmental water.

In this study, CeO2 QDs were synthesized by a simple sol-gel method using Ce(NO3)3·6H2O as raw material and successfully utilized in the determination of 4-NP. Compared with the reported fluorescence method, this probe based on the platform of CeO2 QDs has a high sensitivity for 4-NP with a LOD as low as 1.50 nM. This probe exhibits excellent selectivity for 4-NP over other analogcompounds, particularly 2-NP and 3-NP. As expected, the fluorescence of CeO2 QDs is quenched by 4-NP through the FRET mechanism. This finding is confirmed by the fluorescence lifetime analysis. This method is successfully applied for the analysis of trace 4-NP spiked in the environment water withsatisfactoryresults indicating that CeO2 QDs have the potential to be used as a preliminary detecting probe of 4-NP in real water.

Hongqiang Xu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hailong Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yixia Lu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yanbo Zeng: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yiwen Yang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Zulei Zhang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Hongmei Wang: Writing - review & editing. Xiao Wang: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Lei Li: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Program for Public Welfare Technology of Zhejiang Province (LGF20B050001), Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY20B050009), and Program for Science and Technology of Jiaxing City (2018AY11002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120115.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental persistence, hazard, and mitigation challenges of nitroaromatic compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tiwari,P. Tarale,S. Sivanesan, A. Bafana,Environmental persistence, hazard, and mitigation challenges of nitroaromatic compounds, Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.26 (2019) 28650–28667. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11356-019-06043-8.

Kim, Photocatalytic degradation performance of various types of modified TiO2 against nitrophenols in aqueous systems, J. Clean. Prod. 231 (2019) 899–912. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2019.05.292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of various deep eutectic solvents on the sustainable synthesis of MgFe2O4 nanoparticles for simultaneous electrochemical determination of nitrofurantoin and 4-nitrophenol

George, Effect of various deep eutectic solvents on the sustainable synthesis of MgFe2O4 nanoparticles for simultaneous electrochemical determination of nitrofurantoin and 4‑nitrophenol, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 8 (2020) 1479−1486. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssuschemeng.9b05755.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of 4-nitrophenol in environmental water samples using porous graphitic carbon nitride-coated screen-printed electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ramalingam, V. K. Ponnusamy, S. N.
